content:
# Leviticus Chapter 24 — SOPHIA Consensus Translation

**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **Sources:** EMET (Literal), LOGOS (Formal), PNEUMA (Dynamic)
**Hebrew Base:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 72v–73r
**LXX Reference:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 110–111
**Disagreement Log:** `disagreements/LEV_024.md`

---

## Council Notes

Leviticus 24 is structurally one of the most complex chapters in the Holiness Code, fusing permanent ritual prescription with a singular narrative episode that generates case law. The chapter divides into four movements: (1) the menorah oil ordinance (vv. 1–4); (2) the lechem ha-panim ordinance (vv. 5–9); (3) the blasphemy narrative and its derived law (vv. 10–16); (4) the lex talionis interpolation and the principle of equal justice (vv. 17–22); and (5) the execution narrative and compliance formula (v. 23). The structural parallel to Leviticus 10 (narrative interrupting law) and to Numbers 15:32–36 (the sabbath-wood-gatherer case) situates this chapter within the Pentateuch's procedural pattern for unprecedented capital cases: custody (*mishmar*) → divine inquiry → ruling → execution.

The theological architecture of the chapter is deliberate: the perpetual lamp (*tamid*, vv. 2–4) and the perpetual bread (*tamid*, v. 8) establish the continuous, unceasing presence of the LORD in the sanctuary and the ongoing covenant bond between the LORD and Israel. Against this backdrop, the blasphemy of the divine Name is not incidental impiety; it is a direct assault on the relational fabric that the sanctuary rituals maintain. The lex talionis passage (vv. 17–22) and the declaration of one law for sojourner and native (*mishpat echad*, v. 22) extend the sanctuary's logic of ordered holiness into civil life: as there is one God and one sanctuary, so there is one standard of justice.

The blasphemer's identity as the son of an Israelite woman and an Egyptian man (ben-ishah Yisra'elit / ben-ish Mitsri) is narratively significant: his mixed parentage creates a legal question (does the full force of the blasphemy law apply to him?), and the divine ruling answers it definitively — the law is one for all who dwell within the community, regardless of ancestry. The naming of his mother — Shelomith daughter of Dibri, of the tribe of Dan — is the only named laywoman in all of Leviticus.

Three key Hebrew verbal tensions are preserved in the consensus: (a) *va-yiqqov / noqev* (n-q-v, "to pierce/blaspheme") is rendered "blasphemed" / "blasphemes," following the narrative's own interpretive context; (b) *qillel / yeqallel* (q-l-l, "to curse/make contemptible") is rendered "cursed" / "curses," preserving the distinction from *naqav* in both v. 11 and the general laws of vv. 15–16; (c) *mishpat echad* is rendered "one law" in v. 22, preserving the theological weight of the declaration.

**Key term rulings:** YHWH = "the LORD" | bney yisrael = "sons of Israel" | Moshe = "Moses" | Aharon = "Aaron" | Shelomit bat Divri = "Shelomith daughter of Dibri" | ger = "sojourner" | ezrach = "native" | lechem ha-panim = "bread of the Presence" (not rendered in this passage by name, but "before the LORD" preserved throughout) | mishpat echad = "one law" | mot yumat = "shall surely be put to death" | tamid = "continually" | chuqqat olam = "a statute forever" | berit olam = "a covenant forever"

---

## Consensus Translation

**24:1** And the LORD spoke to Moses, saying:

**24:2** "Command the sons of Israel that they bring to you pure beaten olive oil for the light, to cause a lamp to go up continually.

**24:3** Outside the veil of the testimony, in the tent of meeting, Aaron shall arrange it from evening to morning before the LORD continually. It shall be a statute forever throughout your generations.

**24:4** He shall arrange the lamps on the pure lampstand before the LORD continually.

**24:5** "And you shall take fine flour and bake twelve loaves from it; two-tenths of an ephah shall be in each loaf.

**24:6** And you shall set them in two rows, six to a row, on the pure table before the LORD.

**24:7** And you shall place pure frankincense upon each row, that it may serve as a memorial portion for the bread, a fire-offering to the LORD.

**24:8** Every Sabbath day he shall arrange it before the LORD continually, from the sons of Israel — a covenant forever.

**24:9** And it shall belong to Aaron and his sons, and they shall eat it in a holy place, for it is most holy to him from the fire-offerings of the LORD — a statute forever.

**24:10** Now the son of a woman of Israel went out — and his father was an Egyptian man — among the sons of Israel. And the son of the Israelite woman and a man of Israel strove together in the camp.

**24:11** And the son of the Israelite woman blasphemed the Name and cursed. So they brought him to Moses. His mother's name was Shelomith, the daughter of Dibri, of the tribe of Dan.

**24:12** And they placed him under guard, until the will of the LORD should be made plain to them.

**24:13** And the LORD spoke to Moses, saying:

**24:14** "Bring out the one who cursed to the outside of the camp, and let all who heard him lay their hands on his head, and let the entire congregation stone him.

**24:15** And you shall speak to the sons of Israel, saying: Anyone who curses his God shall bear his sin.

**24:16** Whoever blasphemes the name of the LORD shall surely be put to death. All the congregation shall stone him. The sojourner as well as the native — when he blasphemes the Name, he shall be put to death.

**24:17** "Whoever takes a human life shall surely be put to death.

**24:18** Whoever takes the life of an animal shall make restitution for it — life for life.

**24:19** If anyone inflicts a blemish on his neighbor, as he has done it shall be done to him:

**24:20** fracture for fracture, eye for eye, tooth for tooth. As he inflicted a blemish on a person, so shall it be inflicted on him.

**24:21** Whoever kills an animal shall make restitution for it, and whoever kills a person shall be put to death.

**24:22** You shall have one law for the sojourner and for the native, for I am the LORD your God."

**24:23** And Moses spoke to the sons of Israel, and they brought the one who had cursed to the outside of the camp and stoned him with stones. And the sons of Israel did as the LORD had commanded Moses.

---

## Verse-by-Verse Ruling Notes

**V. 1 — Address formula.** EMET: "spoke to Moshe." LOGOS: "spoke to Moses." PNEUMA: combines vv. 1–2 in a single block. Consensus: "Moses" — English name form per project convention. EMET's "Moshe" is the Hebrew form; project standard for the consensus is the English equivalent throughout.

**V. 2 — "Command the sons of Israel."** EMET: "Command [tsav] the sons of Yisra'el." LOGOS: "Command the sons of Israel that they bring to you." PNEUMA: "Command the Israelites to bring you." Consensus: "Command the sons of Israel that they bring to you" — LOGOS. EMET's "Yisra'el" is a transliteration convention not used in the consensus. PNEUMA's "Israelites" is acceptable but loses the covenant-community formula "sons of Israel" (*bney yisrael*), which is the project-established term.

**V. 2 — "Pure beaten olive oil."** All three agents agree: "pure beaten olive oil" (*shemen zayit zakh katit*). Consensus: "pure beaten olive oil" — unanimous.

**V. 2 — "To cause a lamp to go up" vs. "to cause a lamp to burn."** EMET: "to cause a lamp to go up [le-ha'alot ner]." LOGOS: "to cause a lamp to burn." PNEUMA: "so that the lamp can be kept burning." Consensus: "to cause a lamp to go up" — EMET. The Hiphil of *'alah* ("to cause to ascend/go up") is the technical idiom for tending the sanctuary lamp. LOGOS's "burn" and PNEUMA's "kept burning" are interpretive glosses on the verb's intent; "go up" preserves the technical sense of the flame being "raised" and matches the project's practice of preserving distinct cultic terms.

**V. 3 — "Outside the veil of the testimony."** EMET: "outside the veil of the testimony [mi-chutz la-parokhet ha-edut]." LOGOS: "Outside the veil of the testimony." PNEUMA: "outside the curtain that screens the Covenant chest." Consensus: "Outside the veil of the testimony" — EMET/LOGOS. PNEUMA's "curtain that screens the Covenant chest" is explanatory paraphrase; "the veil of the testimony" preserves the technical sanctuary vocabulary (*parokhet ha-edut*).

**V. 3 — "Aaron shall arrange it."** All three agents agree on "Aaron shall arrange it." Consensus: unanimous.

**V. 3 — "A statute forever throughout your generations" vs. "a permanent ordinance."** EMET: "a statute forever [chuqqat olam] throughout your generations." LOGOS: "It shall be a statute forever throughout your generations." PNEUMA: "This is a permanent ordinance for all your generations." Consensus: "It shall be a statute forever throughout your generations" — LOGOS. PNEUMA's "permanent ordinance" loses the specific term *chuqqat olam*, established as a key phrase in the Holiness Code (cf. Lev 16, 17, 23). "Statute forever" is the project standard for *chuqqat olam*.

**V. 4 — "The pure lampstand" vs. "the pure gold lampstand."** EMET: "the pure lampstand [ha-menorah ha-tehorah]." LOGOS: "the pure gold lampstand." PNEUMA: "the pure gold lampstand." Consensus: "the pure lampstand" — EMET. The MT text at v. 4 reads *ha-menorah ha-tehorah* — "the pure lampstand" without the word "gold" (*zahav*). LOGOS and PNEUMA add "gold," apparently harmonizing with Exod 25:31 and the LOGOS note (which cites *menorat ha-zahav ha-tahor* — but this is LOGOS's rendering for a variant reading); the MT at v. 4 lacks the gold specification, and the consensus follows the MT precisely. The menorah's gold character is implicit; the explicit addition of "gold" here is an harmonistic insertion not supported by the MT at this verse.

**V. 5 — "Two-tenths of an ephah shall be in each loaf."** EMET: "two-tenths [shnei esronim] shall be in the one loaf." LOGOS: "two tenths of an ephah shall be in each loaf." PNEUMA: "using two-tenths of an ephah for each loaf." Consensus: "two-tenths of an ephah shall be in each loaf" — LOGOS. Adding "of an ephah" is standard clarification for the *esron* measure (one-tenth of an ephah); EMET's literal "the one loaf" is a wooden rendering of *ha-challah ha-echat*; "each loaf" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) is idiomatic.

**V. 6 — "Two rows, six to a row" vs. "six in a row."** EMET: "two rows [shetei ma'arakbot], six to a row [sheshet ha-ma'arakah]." LOGOS: "two rows, six in a row." PNEUMA: "two rows, six in each row." Consensus: "two rows, six to a row" — EMET. The MT's *sheshet ha-ma'arakah* (literally "six the row") is an idiomatic phrase meaning "six per row." "Six to a row" preserves the slightly terse Hebrew idiom better than "six in a row" (which could mean one after another) or "six in each row" (PNEUMA, which adds "each"). The distinction is minor, but "to a row" most accurately renders the partitive sense.

**V. 6 — "The pure table" vs. "the pure gold table."** EMET: "the pure table [ha-shulchan ha-tahor]." LOGOS: "the pure gold table." PNEUMA: "the pure gold table." Consensus: "the pure table" — EMET. The MT reads *ha-shulchan ha-tahor* — "the pure table" without *zahav* (gold). Parallel to the ruling at v. 4 on the menorah, LOGOS and PNEUMA again add "gold" from the harmonistic tradition; the MT at this verse does not include it. The consensus follows the MT.

**V. 7 — "Upon each row" vs. "on each row."** EMET: "upon each row [al-ha-ma'arakah]." LOGOS: "on each row." PNEUMA: "alongside each row." Consensus: "upon each row" — EMET/LOGOS. The preposition *al* means "upon/on." PNEUMA's "alongside" imports an interpretive positioning not required by the Hebrew; "upon" is the more literal rendering.

**V. 7 — "That it may serve as a memorial portion for the bread" vs. "that it may go with the bread as a memorial portion."** EMET: "it shall be for the bread as a memorial portion [le-azkarah], a fire-offering to the LORD." LOGOS: "that it may go with the bread as a memorial portion, a food offering to the LORD." PNEUMA: "This is to serve as a token portion, a fire-offering to the LORD in place of the bread." Consensus: "that it may serve as a memorial portion for the bread, a fire-offering to the LORD" — adjusted. The MT reads *ve-hayetah la-lechem le-azkarah isheh la-YHWH* — "and it shall be for the bread as a memorial portion, a fire-offering to the LORD." "Serve as" captures the functional Hebrew *hayetah* better than LOGOS's "go with." PNEUMA's "token portion" for *azkarah* is interpretive; "memorial portion" is the established project rendering (cf. Lev 2 series). LOGOS's "food offering" for *isheh* is weak — the established project term is "fire-offering" (*isheh*).

**V. 8 — "Every Sabbath day" — doubling.** EMET: "on the sabbath day, on the sabbath day [be-yom ha-shabbat be-yom ha-shabbat]." LOGOS: "Every Sabbath day." PNEUMA: "Every Sabbath." Consensus: "Every Sabbath day" — LOGOS. The Hebrew doubles the phrase for emphasis ("on the Sabbath day, on the Sabbath day"), but the standard idiomatic rendering of this emphatic doubling is "Every Sabbath day." EMET's literal repetition is accurate but creates awkward English; "Every Sabbath day" (LOGOS) captures the force without the repetition. PNEUMA drops "day."

**V. 8 — "From the sons of Israel — a covenant forever."** EMET: "from the sons of Yisra'el — an everlasting covenant [berit olam]." LOGOS: "it is from the sons of Israel as a covenant forever." PNEUMA: "this is an everlasting covenant, a commitment on the part of the Israelites." Consensus: "from the sons of Israel — a covenant forever" — adjusted. The MT reads *me-et benei Yisra'el berit olam* — "from the sons of Israel, an everlasting covenant." LOGOS's "as a covenant forever" and EMET's "everlasting covenant" both work; the project uses "a covenant forever" for *berit olam* here (parallel to "a statute forever" for *chuqqat olam*, maintaining the -forever pattern). PNEUMA's "a commitment on the part of the Israelites" is a loose paraphrase that loses the covenant-formula weight of *berit olam*. "Sons of Israel" is preserved over PNEUMA's "Israelites."

**V. 9 — "Most holy to him" vs. "most holy for him."** EMET: "for it is most holy for him [ki qodesh qodashim hu lo]." LOGOS: "for it is most holy to him." PNEUMA: "for it is most holy — his portion." Consensus: "for it is most holy to him" — LOGOS. The MT *qodesh qodashim hu lo* — "holiness of holinesses, it is to/for him" — uses *lo* (to/for him). "To him" (LOGOS) is slightly more natural in English than "for him" (EMET) in a statement about the priestly share. PNEUMA's "his portion" is a paraphrase; *lo* alone does not specify "portion."

**V. 9 — "From the fire-offerings of the LORD" vs. "from the LORD's food offerings."** EMET: "from the fire-offerings of the LORD [me-ishei YHWH]." LOGOS: "from the LORD's food offerings." PNEUMA: "of the fire-offerings of the LORD." Consensus: "from the fire-offerings of the LORD" — EMET/PNEUMA. The MT *me-ishei YHWH* — the *isheh* is the established project term "fire-offering" (as in Lev 1–7). LOGOS's "food offering" is a non-standard rendering of *isheh*; the root's sense of fire (*esh*) is retained in "fire-offering" throughout the project.

**V. 9 — "A statute forever" vs. "a perpetual due."** EMET: "a statute forever [chuqqat olam]." LOGOS: "a perpetual due." PNEUMA: "given to him as a permanent right." Consensus: "a statute forever" — EMET. The MT has *chuqqat olam* at the end of v. 9, matching the identical phrase at v. 3. LOGOS's "perpetual due" and PNEUMA's "permanent right" are paraphrases; the established project rendering is "a statute forever."

**V. 10 — Opening narrative construction.** EMET: "And the son of a woman of Yisra'el went out... and he was the son of an Egyptian man... among the sons of Yisra'el; and the son of the woman of Yisra'el... and a man of Yisra'el strove together." LOGOS: "Now a man who was the son of an Israelite woman went out — and his father was an Egyptian man — among the sons of Israel. And the son of the Israelite woman and a man of Israel quarreled in the camp." PNEUMA: "Now a man whose mother was an Israelite and whose father was an Egyptian came out among the Israelites. In the camp, a fight broke out between this man of mixed descent and an Israelite." Consensus: LOGOS base with "strove together" — see next ruling.

**V. 10 — "Strove together" vs. "quarreled" vs. "a fight broke out."** EMET: "strove together [va-yinnatsu]." LOGOS: "quarreled." PNEUMA: "a fight broke out." Consensus: "strove together" — EMET. The MT *va-yinnatsu* (Niphal of *n-ts-h*) denotes a fight or violent quarrel; the same verb appears in Exod 2:13 ("two Hebrews striving together"). "Quarreled" (LOGOS) is slightly too mild; "strove together" preserves the sense of physical contention implied by the Niphal of *natsa*. PNEUMA's "a fight broke out" avoids naming the individuals as the agents, which weakens the narrative construction. The consensus uses: "And the son of the Israelite woman and a man of Israel strove together in the camp."

**V. 10 — "Woman of Israel" vs. "Israelite woman."** EMET: "son of a woman of Yisra'el [ben-ishah Yisra'elit]." LOGOS: "son of an Israelite woman." PNEUMA: "man whose mother was an Israelite." Consensus: "son of a woman of Israel" — adjusted toward EMET but with English name form. The MT *ben-ishah Yisra'elit* is literally "son of a woman, an Israelite-woman." "Israelite woman" (LOGOS) and "a woman of Israel" are equivalent; the consensus uses "a woman of Israel" at the first mention and "the Israelite woman" subsequently, matching the MT's shift from the first introduction to the repeated reference.

**V. 11 — "Blasphemed the Name and cursed."** EMET: "blasphemed the Name [va-yiqqov... et-ha-Shem] and he cursed [va-yeqallel]." LOGOS: "blasphemed the Name and cursed." PNEUMA: "blasphemed the Name and cursed it." Consensus: "blasphemed the Name and cursed" — EMET/LOGOS. PNEUMA adds "it" after "cursed" — the MT has no object after *va-yeqallel*; the object of the cursing is left grammatically open. The addition of "it" (PNEUMA) implies the Name was both blasphemed and cursed, which may be correct theologically but is not present in the Hebrew text. The objectless "cursed" is preserved.

**V. 11 — "Shelomith, the daughter of Dibri" — name forms.** EMET: "Shelomit [שְׁלֹמִית], daughter of Divri [דִּבְרִי]." LOGOS: "Shelomith, the daughter of Dibri." PNEUMA: "Shelomith daughter of Dibri." Consensus: "Shelomith, the daughter of Dibri" — LOGOS. English name form: "Shelomith" is the standard English rendering of the Hebrew *Shlomit*; "Dibri" is the standard rendering of *Divri*. The project uses English name forms throughout.

**V. 12 — "Under guard" vs. "in custody."** EMET: "under guard [ba-mishmar]." LOGOS: "in custody." PNEUMA: "in custody." Consensus: "under guard" — EMET. The MT *ba-mishmar* is literally "in the place of guarding/watching." "Under guard" preserves the literal meaning of *mishmar* (from *shamar*, "to watch/guard") and is more precise than the general "in custody," which imports a more modern institutional connotation.

**V. 12 — "Until the will of the LORD should be made plain to them."** EMET: "to make a decision [lifros lahem] according to the mouth of the LORD [al-pi YHWH]." LOGOS: "until the will of the LORD should be plain to them." PNEUMA: "while they waited to hear what the LORD would say." Consensus: "until the will of the LORD should be made plain to them" — LOGOS adjusted. The MT phrase *lifros lahem al-pi YHWH* is a purpose clause: "to make clear / explain to them according to the mouth of the LORD." LOGOS's "until the will of the LORD should be plain to them" is the most idiomatic rendering. PNEUMA's "to hear what the LORD would say" is colloquial and loses the specific verb *paras* ("to make distinct/explain"). The consensus adopts LOGOS with the addition of "made" for clarity.

**V. 13 — Address formula.** All three agents agree: "And the LORD spoke to Moses, saying." Consensus: unanimous.

**V. 14 — "Bring out the one who cursed" vs. "bring the one who has cursed."** EMET: "Bring out [hotse] the one who cursed [ha-meqallel]." LOGOS: "Bring the one who has cursed outside the camp." PNEUMA: "Bring the one who has done the cursing outside the camp." Consensus: "Bring out the one who cursed to the outside of the camp" — EMET with directional preposition. The MT *hotse el-mi-chutz la-machaneh* is a Hiphil imperative with the directional phrase "to the outside of the camp." "Bring out" (EMET) captures the Hiphil causative better than LOGOS's plain "Bring." PNEUMA's "the one who has done the cursing" is a circumlocution for the Hebrew participle *ha-meqallel* ("the curser/the one who cursed").

**V. 14 — "All who heard him" — hand-laying.** EMET: "all those who heard... shall lay their hands upon his head." LOGOS: "all who heard him lay their hands on his head." PNEUMA: "Let all who heard him lay their hands on his head." Consensus: "let all who heard him lay their hands on his head" — LOGOS/PNEUMA. The MT has an imperfect with vav-consecutive (*ve-samkhu*) expressing command: "and all who heard shall lay their hands." The jussive construction "let all who heard him lay their hands" (PNEUMA) renders this as direct instruction and is slightly smoother. EMET's "all those who heard" with the more formal subject is replaced by the more natural "all who heard him."

**V. 14 — "The entire congregation shall stone him."** EMET: "the entire congregation [kol-ha-edah] shall stone him [ragam]." LOGOS: "all the congregation stone him." PNEUMA: "let the whole community stone him." Consensus: "let the entire congregation stone him" — adjusted. Parallel to the jussive construction above. "Congregation" for *edah* is the standard project term (established throughout Leviticus); PNEUMA's "community" is looser. "Entire" (EMET) reflects *kol* ("all/entire").

**V. 15 — "Anyone who curses his God" — subject formulation.** EMET: "Any man [ish ish] who curses his God." LOGOS: "Whoever curses his God." PNEUMA: "Anyone who curses his God." Consensus: "Anyone who curses his God" — PNEUMA/LOGOS. The MT *ish ish ki yeqallel Elohav* uses the distributive *ish ish* ("any man whatsoever"). Following the project ruling at Lev 20:9 (on *ish ish*), the distributive "anyone" captures the universal scope without the slight gender restriction of "any man."

**V. 15 — "Shall bear his sin."** All three agents agree: "shall bear his sin." Consensus: unanimous.

**V. 16 — "Whoever blasphemes the name of the LORD."** EMET: "he who pierces the Name of the LORD [noqev Shem YHWH]." LOGOS: "Whoever blasphemes the name of the LORD." PNEUMA: "Whoever blasphemes the name of the LORD." Consensus: "Whoever blasphemes the name of the LORD" — LOGOS/PNEUMA. EMET's "pierces" preserves the literal *n-q-v* etymology but is unusual English for this clearly juridical statement; "blasphemes" is the established English rendering in this legal context and is what all formal translations use. "The name of the LORD" follows LOGOS/PNEUMA and the MT's *Shem YHWH*.

**V. 16 — "Shall surely be put to death" (mot yumat).** All three agents agree on this formula. Consensus: unanimous — "shall surely be put to death."

**V. 16 — "The sojourner as well as the native."** EMET: "the sojourner as well as the native [ka-ger ka-ezrach]." LOGOS: "The sojourner as well as the native." PNEUMA: "Whether he is a foreigner or a native-born Israelite." Consensus: "the sojourner as well as the native" — EMET/LOGOS. "Sojourner" for *ger* and "native" for *ezrach* are the established project terms. PNEUMA's "foreigner" for *ger* is too strong an equivalence — *ger* is a resident alien who lives within the community, not a foreigner outside it. "Native-born Israelite" adds "Israelite" beyond the Hebrew *ezrach* (which means simply the native-born person of the land).

**V. 16 — "When he blasphemes the Name, he shall be put to death."** EMET: "when he blasphemes the Name [be-noqvo et-ha-Shem] he shall be put to death." LOGOS: "when he blasphemes the Name, shall be put to death." PNEUMA: "when he blasphemes the Name, he must die." Consensus: "when he blasphemes the Name, he shall be put to death" — EMET/adjusted. The MT's closing clause is preserved with the explicit subject "he" (EMET, which supplies it naturally from the Hebrew suffix). "Shall be put to death" matches the *yumat* formula consistently used throughout.

**V. 17 — "Whoever takes a human life" vs. "any man who strikes fatally."** EMET: "a man [ve-ish] who strikes fatally any person [ki yakkeh kol-nefesh adam]." LOGOS: "Whoever takes a human life." PNEUMA: "Whoever takes a human life." Consensus: "Whoever takes a human life" — LOGOS/PNEUMA. The MT *ki yakkeh kol-nefesh adam* uses the Hiphil of *n-k-h* ("to strike, strike fatally") with *kol-nefesh adam* ("any soul of a human being"). LOGOS/PNEUMA's "takes a human life" is idiomatic; EMET's "strikes fatally any person" is more literal. "Takes a human life" is standard formal English for this capital offense and is preferred for the consensus over the awkward "strikes fatally."

**V. 18 — "Whoever takes the life of an animal" vs. "the one who strikes fatally an animal."** EMET: "the one who strikes fatally an animal [u-makke nefesh-behemah]." LOGOS: "Whoever takes an animal's life." PNEUMA: "Whoever kills another person's animal." Consensus: "Whoever takes the life of an animal" — adjusted from LOGOS. The MT *u-makke nefesh-behemah* literally "the one who strikes the life of an animal." The parallel construction with v. 17 ("takes a human life") recommends "takes the life of an animal" for formal consistency. PNEUMA adds "another person's" — not in the MT at this point.

**V. 18 — "Shall make restitution for it."** EMET: "shall make restitution for it [yeshallemennah]." LOGOS: "shall make it good." PNEUMA: "must make full restitution." Consensus: "shall make restitution for it" — EMET. The Pi'el of *shillem* is the standard restitution term in the Holiness Code; "make restitution" is the established project rendering. LOGOS's "make it good" is acceptable but less precise. PNEUMA adds "full" which has no basis in the MT here.

**V. 18 — "Life for life."** All three agents agree: "life for life." Consensus: unanimous.

**V. 19 — "Inflicts a blemish" vs. "injures."** EMET: "inflicts a blemish [yitten mum] in his fellow [ba-amito]." LOGOS: "injures his neighbor." PNEUMA: "injures his neighbor." Consensus: "inflicts a blemish on his neighbor" — EMET adjusted. The MT *yitten mum ba-amito* is literally "gives/inflicts a *mum* (blemish/defect) upon his fellow." "Blemish" (*mum*) is the technical term used throughout Leviticus for physical defects that disqualify from sacrifice and priestly service (cf. Lev 21); its use here is deliberate and theologically significant. LOGOS/PNEUMA's "injures" is too general. "Neighbor" is used for *amito* as the more natural English equivalent of *amit* ("fellow/associate/neighbor").

**V. 19 — "As he has done it shall be done to him."** EMET: "as he did [ka'asher asah], so shall it be done to him [ken ye'aseh lo]." LOGOS: "as he has done it shall be done to him." PNEUMA: "whatever he has done must be done to him in return." Consensus: "as he has done it shall be done to him" — LOGOS. Standard formal rendering of the talionic equivalence formula.

**V. 20 — The lex talionis triad.** EMET: "Fracture for fracture [shever tachat shaver], eye for eye [ayin tachat ayin], tooth for tooth [shen tachat shen]." LOGOS: "fracture for fracture, eye for eye, tooth for tooth." PNEUMA: "fracture for fracture, eye for eye, tooth for tooth." Consensus: unanimous on the formula.

**V. 20 — "As he inflicted a blemish on a person, so shall it be inflicted on him."** EMET: "as he inflicted a blemish upon a person [ka'asher yitten mum ba-adam], so shall it be inflicted upon him [ken yinnaten bo]." LOGOS: "Whatever injury he has given a person shall be given to him." PNEUMA: "The same injury he has inflicted on someone else must be inflicted on him." Consensus: "As he inflicted a blemish on a person, so shall it be inflicted on him" — EMET adjusted. The MT uses *mum* again at v. 20 (matching v. 19); "blemish" should be preserved in the consensus for terminological consistency with v. 19. LOGOS's "injury" and PNEUMA's "injury" both substitute a general term for the specific *mum*. The passive *yinnaten bo* is rendered "shall it be inflicted on him" to match the passive of the preceding talionic clauses.

**V. 21 — Summary statements.** EMET: "he who strikes an animal shall make restitution for it [u-makke behemah yeshallemennah], and he who strikes a person shall be put to death [u-makke adam yumat]." LOGOS: "Whoever kills an animal shall make it good, and whoever kills a person shall be put to death." PNEUMA: "Whoever kills an animal must make full restitution; whoever kills a person must be put to death." Consensus: "Whoever kills an animal shall make restitution for it, and whoever kills a person shall be put to death." LOGOS adjusted. "Kills" is an acceptable English equivalent for *n-k-h* in this summary context (matching the natural pair "kills an animal / kills a person"). "Make restitution for it" (EMET) over LOGOS's "make it good" — for consistency with the established project term at v. 18. PNEUMA's "full" is again an addition.

**V. 22 — "One law."** EMET: "one law [mishpat echad]." LOGOS: "one law." PNEUMA: "one standard of justice." Consensus: "one law" — EMET/LOGOS. The MT *mishpat echad* is the chapter's theological climax. "One law" is the literal rendering and preserves the weight of the declaration. PNEUMA's "one standard of justice" is a paraphrase that expands the Hebrew; *mishpat* alone carries the force of "law/ruling/judgment" and does not require the addition of "standard of."

**V. 22 — "For the sojourner and for the native."** All three agents preserve "sojourner" and "native" (LOGOS/EMET) or "foreigner" and "native-born" (PNEUMA). Consensus: "for the sojourner and for the native" — EMET/LOGOS, per the ruling at v. 16 on *ger* and *ezrach*.

**V. 23 — "Stoned him with stones" vs. "stoned him."** EMET: "stoned him with stones [va-yirgemu oto even]." LOGOS: "stoned him with stones." PNEUMA: "stoned him." Consensus: "stoned him with stones" — EMET/LOGOS. The MT *even* ("stone") is present as the instrument-accusative; "with stones" is in the Hebrew and should be preserved. PNEUMA drops it.

**V. 23 — Closing compliance formula.** EMET: "the sons of Yisra'el did as the LORD commanded Moshe." LOGOS: "the sons of Israel did as the LORD had commanded Moses." PNEUMA: "The Israelites did exactly what the LORD had commanded Moses." Consensus: "the sons of Israel did as the LORD had commanded Moses" — LOGOS. "Sons of Israel" is the project term over PNEUMA's "Israelites." PNEUMA adds "exactly" — not present in the MT. The standard compliance formula is preserved as-is.

---

## Textual Notes for the Consensus Record

**On the chapter's structural unity:** The combination of permanent ritual prescription (vv. 1–9) with narrative-generated law (vv. 10–23) reflects a compositional technique seen elsewhere in Leviticus (chs. 10, 16) and Numbers (ch. 15). The function is not accidental: the permanent sanctuary rituals — the lamp that never goes out, the bread that is always renewed — establish the ongoing sacred presence of the LORD as the context within which the blasphemy narrative becomes intelligible. The chapter that begins with *tamid* (perpetual) ends with perfect obedience (*ka'asher tsivvah YHWH* — "as the LORD commanded"); the perpetual quality of the sanctuary service and the immediate, complete obedience of the community form a single covenantal arc.

**On *va-yiqqov* / *noqev* (vv. 11, 16) — "blasphemed" vs. "pierced":** The verb *n-q-v* (Qal) has a physical primary meaning ("to pierce/bore") that extends to verbal utterance in this unique legal context. EMET's "pierces the Name" at v. 16 is etymologically precise but is unusual English in a legal formula. The consensus adopts "blasphemes" for the legal formulations at v. 16, while the narrative at v. 11 uses "blasphemed" throughout, consistent with the LXX's *eponomasas* (having named/invoked) which confirms the act was one of speech. The two verbs *naqav* and *qillel* are distinguished throughout: "blasphemed" for *naqav* and "cursed" for *qillel*.

**On *lechem ha-panim* (vv. 5–9):** The term "bread of the Presence" (*lechem ha-panim*) does not appear explicitly in the Hebrew text of this chapter; the text refers simply to the *challot* (loaves) set *lifnei YHWH* ("before the LORD"). The consensus preserves "before the LORD" throughout vv. 5–9 rather than importing the explicit *lechem ha-panim* designation from Exod 25:30. The bread's identity as the bread of the Presence is clear from context; the consensus does not add the designation in the verse text.

**On *berit olam* (v. 8) and *chuqqat olam* (vv. 3, 9):** Both permanent-ordinance formulas appear in this chapter. *Berit olam* ("a covenant forever") appears only once in Leviticus at this verse — elevating the weekly bread-arrangement to covenantal status equal to the Noahic and Abrahamic covenants. *Chuqqat olam* ("a statute forever") appears at vv. 3 and 9. The consensus renders each formula literally: "a covenant forever" (v. 8) and "a statute forever" (vv. 3, 9).

**On the lex talionis (vv. 17–22):** The three occurrences of *tachat* ("in place of/instead of") in the talionic triad (v. 20) are rendered "for" throughout — the standard English equivalent of the substitution principle. The consensus follows EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA's unanimous agreement on the formula. The distinction between the animal-injury law (financial restitution, vv. 18, 21) and the human-injury law (equivalent retaliation, vv. 19–20) is preserved; the consensus does not adjudicate between literal and monetary application, as the Hebrew text itself does not specify the mode of enforcement.

**On *mishpat echad* (v. 22) — theological significance:** The declaration "one law" as the chapter's climax makes the legal principle the culmination of both the ritual legislation and the narrative. The singularity of the law (*mishpat echad*) mirrors the singularity of the LORD (*YHWH echad* — Deut 6:4), whose self-identification grounds the declaration: "for I am the LORD your God." This is the standard Holiness Code closure formula (cf. Lev 18:2; 19:2; 20:24, 26), and here it functions as the ultimate warrant for equal justice: one God, therefore one law.

**On LXX divergences (Vaticanus pp. 110–111):** (1) At v. 7, the LXX adds "and you shall place on each row salt" — a plus not in the MT, likely a harmonization with the salt requirement for grain offerings (Lev 2:13). The consensus follows the MT and does not incorporate this addition. (2) At v. 11, the LXX uses *eponomasas* ("having named/invoked") for *va-yiqqov* (*naqav*), and *katarasato* ("cursed") for *va-yeqallel* (*qillel*) — confirming the dual-verb structure of the MT. (3) At v. 16, the LXX (Vaticanus) appears to omit the final clause "when he blasphemes the Name, he shall be put to death" — a partial haplography of the MT's double formulation. The MT's double formulation is preserved in the consensus.

---

*SOPHIA consensus completed. Leviticus 24:1–23. All 23 verses rendered. Source arbiter rulings logged in `disagreements/LEV_024.md`.*
